title: Sam Abrahams -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Sam Abrahams -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Hi, Sam.
Sorry I'm so late.
Oh, my, Andi.
No, that's fine.
I was just about to send a message to Marilia because I get super paranoid that...
Um, I've got the time zone wrong or something.
Oh, no, it's me.
Sorry.
That's totally fine.
That's okay.
How are you?
I'm good.
How are you?
I'm all right, thank you.
Where are you calling from?
So I'm in Guadalajara in Jalisco.
Oh, cool.
But I'm from the UK, so, but that's where I am.
Yes.
And where are you, sorry?
Uh, I'm on the east coast of the US, so I am in Rhode Island.
Okay, so, was it, was it 1120 for you?
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah, so we're two hours apart at the moment, but, um, the clocks don't change here, so when, I guess, like, next week or the week after, when your clocks change, I don't know what I think.
Yeah.
Uh, I grew up in the Caribbean where the clocks also don't change, so I have to remember that.
Where in the Caribbean did you grow up?
St.
Thomas in the Virgin Islands.
Goodness me.
I bet that's beautiful.
Do you still have time there?
Yeah, my parents still live there.
Oh, my goodness.
Wow.
Yeah.
It was beautiful.
It was quite warm.
It's the first really cold day today.
I'm remembering that.
Cool.
Well, we can get started.
Pretty standard interview format.
I can kind of give you a sense of what we're looking for in the role, and then we can talk about you, and then I'll try and leave, like, five minutes at the end for you to ask me any questions.
Okay, great.
Perfect.
So this role, the managing editor, I like to think of them as kind of, like, the core project manager and owner of all the deliverables for client, for, like, a group of clients in the delivery.
So you're managing like three to five clients, the goal is to get to five, and we're growing quite quickly.
So I think we will, it will be there sooner than we think.
And you're working with content managers.
Well, today you're working with content managers, though, as we evolve, you may be also responsible for developing content within our core platform.
So we, the growthx model is a like services agency model.
But when we think about, you know, kind of our business long term, we do have a product platform on the back end that all of our delivery team uses to generate the content using AI workflows.
It's called Atlas.
So have you talked to Panzer already about that?
Matthew, yeah.
Matthew, terms at this to me, yes.
Okay, yes.
Okay.
Panzer's like our Atlas expert, I think more about like the team structure, operations, and kind of like the how, and Panzer thinks about the what.

So yeah, so you're, you know, you will be working in Atlas in the kind of like helping generate the content, and responsible for making sure that on a week-to-week basis, we're meeting contractual obligations.

We aim for five articles per week for our clients, and then you're working with an account strategist or an account director to, you know, think about long-term, what's the direction of the, you know, what's the direction that we're going in terms of the relationship, in terms of the client's goals, helping set like strategy, usually on a quarterly basis, as though reset strategy.
And helping think about kind of like optimization, SEO, and AEO optimization.
So that's the role, and we are a startup, so it will probably change, just acknowledging.
But I think like the core things that we're looking for are like that project management ability, and like kind of organization, and keeping things on track, and like editorial taste.
So can you both, you know, edit and can you recognize what's good?
How do you develop what's good?
Can you express that?
So those are the things that we're looking for as we talk about this role.
Understood.
Yes, that's pretty much in line with what I've understood so far, guess, about the role.
Yes.
Perfect.
Cool.
Well, yeah, let's talk about you.
Would love to get your elevator pitch.
And then I have some questions that we can
I'm not quite sure I've prepared an elevator pitch, but to let you know who I am, as I've pretty much explained to Marilia and Matthew or Panda, I'm at heart a content editor.
So I see myself as someone who helps writers or helps clients and kind of bridges that gap between them to make sure that whatever we deliver to a client meets or surpasses those client expectations.

And even when those client expectations aren't maybe clearly defined, I will help define them, I'll understand what their core goals are, and help to shape our content, our deliverables, according to those within our scope.

As an editor, I've helped develop strategies, and I've looked, I conduct SERP analysis, competitor analysis, I have to understand the product, like I say, I have to understand the client goals, of course, not just client goals, but also just actually, I don't know how applicable this is with how growthx works, but also just the kind of pressures that our clients experience, whether those are the kinds of internal pressures that they might experience, and there may be things that I can do to help alleviate those, and also speak through sometimes our point of contact to the higher-ups, so that we're kind of always focused on retention, basically, because retention is,
Really, that's kind of like the end.
Yes, for us as well.

But also within that capacity, as well as like the content management and that kind of client relationship or client understanding side, I also help to manage those deliverables and ensure that they get delivered on time, of course.
So in my most recent job, in my most recent role at Flying Cat Marketing, that was some of my work.
But in previous roles, that's been a larger part of what I've done as a project manager.
Yeah.
Got it.
So not an elevator pitch, because I'm pretty sure those are supposed to be like about half a minute or something.
No, I know.
But like the high level overview, right?
The, the, the, the quick view.
Um, yeah.
Talk to me about like, um.
Managing multiple accounts or clients, and how you work through overlapping deadlines, what tools you use, things like that.
Okay, so normally, I mean, one of the kind of, like, especially in the startup, one of, like, the challenges that you face is what you're describing.
And the goal that you have is to have a systemized operation at work.
So, in fact, as a project manager or as an editor, I don't really have to worry about those things because it's all kind of streamlined and set out in place, right?
So, the system will recognize client expectations, the scope of work, what...
The SLA, the system will kind of synthesize all of that, and we'll have our timelines and deadlines set, ideally, in an automated way, according to those things.
And those normally, like, we would establish during the onboarding phase of a client relationship, and that would include how free is our client?
Like, how much time do they want to invest in this project?

So those are the kind of things that I'll be considerate of, and to do that, in, like, my most recent job at ClientCat, we were using platforms like ClickUp and then Productive to manage all the projects that we had in place.
case, would have a Google sheet, which showed all the deliverables for an upcoming quarter.
and that
We'd also show what has the status of those deliverables and the approvals.
We would have, we'd communicate with the client normally using Slack.
And we would have zap firing messages when, as soon as a deliverable reached its client ready status.
So those are like the three, I guess, or four basic tools that we're using to manage deliverables and ensure that we're meeting deadlines and client expectations.
Yeah, okay.
I mean, yeah, for us, it's because the delivery cadence, it is a little bit relentless.
It's, you know, five a week for, you know, so you're editing between 15 and 25 pieces of content a week.
So, yeah.
If you were faced with a situation like that, maybe how would you approach organizing your time and think about the structure of your week?
Yeah, so, I mean, you have to identify what the bottlenecks are, and manage those first.
Often, a final edit will not be prioritized over, for example, an outline approval, if at GrowthX you meant you have outline approvals, because that's how it will work in previous years.
Yeah, it depends on the client.
Some clients, you know, if we're trying to produce at scale that outline approval is just like another layer.
So we do assignment approval, and once we go through calibration at the beginning, and then once they trust us, we usually don't do an outline approval.
Right, okay, okay.
So if...
Those outline approvals are needed.
Normally, whatever projects I'm working on, normally the client will prefer to have those batched, right?
So they'll prefer to receive, say, four or five, whether it's or outlines to, whether it's final drafts or outlines to approve, they'll prefer to have them in one go.
And that might be, say, on a bi-monthly basis or on a weekly basis.
And so on Monday morning or Tuesday morning, whatever it is, they've got these batches of things to go through.
So the way that I'll work is understanding when each client will expect their batch of work.
Yeah.
Because an outline approval affects the entire workflow, those I might be prioritizing over, say, final edits.
Because once those final edits go through, through.
So Thank you.
And they're approved.
They can be published straight away.
However, of course, we'll have a deadline.
So I'll need to kind of respect that as well.
Now, if I'm editing, what was you said, between like 15 and 25 pieces?
Yeah.
So that's, I mean, when you get up to, say, 25, that's a lot of work.
Yeah.
So I will need to understand, by the time I'm editing 25 pieces of work, I'll need those pieces to largely be really close to going, right, going through.
Yeah.
Yeah.

And so if that's not the case, then I'll need to kind of, there needs to be an adjustment at somewhere along the workflow, because something isn't quite working, which is fine, course, because it's kind of normal, but you need to make that adjustment.
So one of my concerns will be is not just...
It's like getting it client ready, but understanding why, why has it taken me an hour to edit this piece when really I need to be able to edit it in 20, 25 minutes max.
Yeah.
Of course, you know, those are just examples.
It depends on the volume and the nature of the work, course.
Yeah.
Basically.
Yeah, yeah.
And I mean, I think that's something that like we're starting with three and moving towards five.

And ideally, like as you spend more time with the first few clients, you figure out ways that you can streamline the process where, or like improve the prompts and the AI so that you don't have to, yeah, generate as much.
Can you talk to me about a time when you had to quickly shift content strategy for a client?
When you say shift strategy, do you mean turn the strategy into a deliverable?
think of?
Oh.
Oh.
Oh.
Um, so I, I, here's an example, like, we had a client recently, who we had like set out a strategy, we were kind of rolling along.

And then one of their competitors put out a comparison page that was ranking very quickly in AI, like for AI prompts, and it said that there, they were only good for small medium business when really they were enterprise facing.
Um, and so the client kind of panicked and said, we need to, we need to address this right away.
And, you know, that wasn't necessarily like a priority in the content strategy that we had laid out.
Obviously, like we think about competitors and things like that, but like, you know, the competitor put out a piece and the client panicked.
So how would you think about addressing something like that?
Or do you have an example that's similar?
Yeah, that's a really, that's a really, that's interesting example, because it, it, it kind of, it speaks to your ability to perform that analysis.
interesting example.
Like a quick, kind of a quick turnover.
Yeah.
So I don't think I can draw an example quite like that, but there would be occasions where a strategy would be approved, say, in two, say two months in advance of the deliverable.
And then just before we're about to like get to work on that piece, there will be, for whatever reason, someone on the client side will say, oh, no, no, no, no, let's scrap that.
We don't want to talk about this, or this doesn't really match our product in X way, or this is the direction we want to go in.
And I've experienced situations analogous to that, like, you know, lots and lots of times.
I'm trying to say like a quick number of times.
Like that's similar kind of things have happened.
And.
That might be a real problem because suddenly you've got to, okay, in terms of just like the client expectation side, you've got to like replace that piece with something.
Right.
But in terms of the strategy side, you've got to really deliver something that's actually going to help you retain this client.
Yeah.
So that's a situation that we've often faced.
And in that kind of situation, normally say at Flying Cat Marketing, it would be really the strategist's domain.
And I'll just wait for the keyword to drop.
Right.
But in that kind of situation, I might come in and suggest ways that we could tackle that problem.
And to do that, I would say, look, problem challenge.
And to do that, I would like, I would like, look at what else we're producing at that time.
been great.
you'll And
Normally, often as an editor, I have maybe a deeper understanding of the brand and the product and the ICP.
That shouldn't be the case, but it just is.
And so I'd be well positioned to suggest whatever the pivot might be.
So it might be like to add a page to this cluster, or it might be to project forward and say, look, this is a piece that we do.
Or it might be like two optimization pieces where we go to previously published work and say, look, we can we can improve these pieces instead.
So those are maybe examples where I've had to step in and support like a quick turnaround on this strategy shift.
Yeah.
I'm just taking notes.
Okay, and then I think my last big question is just to talk about AI experience.
So.
So, you know, what AI automation tools have you used in your work, and maybe how has that evolved over time?
Okay, so most of the tools that I use on a daily basis now have an AI feature to them.
So, for example, one of the tools that we use at Flying Cat, or I used at Flying Cat, is a platform called Slight.
I don't know if you're familiar with it.
And we'd use that to manage all of our SOPs and processes.
Okay.
And there is an AI feature in that, which is really, really cool, and it allows you to just quickly find the thing that you need.
And that's basically it.
And what we did is we put all of our SOPs and brand Bibles, because I would create a brand Bible for every client, put them all at Slight, and that would make everything super accessible.
So that's one way that we might use AI.
But that's like a very...

Functional thing, also like that content creation process or strategy creation process, primarily I would be using ChatGPT, although I would also use other tools, but not that much, and certainly not in comparison to ChatGPT, that was my go-to.
Okay.

And I would use regularly ChatGPT to help me create heading structures for outlines, to start to check ideas, even to kind of like better understand a kind of vague client comment in a document sometimes.
And I would also use it to help me perform keyword and competitor analysis.
Well, by exporting, say, keyword report from Ahrefs, and also, more recently, I've created GPTs to help me create really complex briefs.
Cool, so you did the exercise, right?
So, how did you use AI in that process?
I used AI, as I explained to Matthew, like, the bulk of my experience of using AI is really as a tool to support kind of domain expertise, and introduce that into my output.
And that was mainly how I was using AI.

ChatGPT, to help me create the brief and article, I wasn't using it to create it with velocity because I was more interested in ensuring just the optimal delivery rather than the optimal delivery time, right?
So I was using it in a very kind of like manualized way.
And what I did do, I mean, among other things, what I did do is I used ChatGPT to create a framework of analysis to help me choose which article I was going to rewrite.
And it gave me five options.
And I then looked at those five options.
It made a recommended one.
I think I chose its second or third recommendation.
But, yeah, it was a very kind of interactive experience, creating, delivering that test.
With the brief, I used it to kind of interpret tone of voice, brand concerns.
I think, I think you gave, there were some materials on, maybe the, I'm not quite sure, on the brand or competitors.
It might be confusing projects.
Yeah, yeah.
No, that's all good.
Well, we'll go deeper into that in the, it's kind of what the second exercise is about.
So I just wanted to kind of stress test that a little bit in terms of how you used it.
I think I'm going to do, well, if I get to the next stage as a presentation, when I do that, I'll kind of go through.
that's exactly what that is.
Yeah.
We've just also had people be like, but I didn't use AI at all.
And then it's like, well, then we can't really move you to the next stage.
So that's why I ask.
Okay.
Well, we have a couple of minutes.
I can go over if you are available since I know I was late.
So I'm happy to answer any questions you have.
Right.
Okay, so the first, you have explained this to some degree, but I'd love to understand a bit more about your role, because where I've worked previously, we haven't had a head of customer operations.
We've had a head of operations who is like concerned with managing the workflows and the tools that we use.
We're not a head of customer operations.
And I think I have a reasonable level of understanding of what that concerns.
But I'd love

Yeah, think the interesting thing about our model is that it's not a straight agency model, and so in consideration of, like, my background is really in customer success and, like, account management, and so I manage, like, the director layer who oversees the pods, and Panzer's really thinking about Atlas itself and the, like, quality of the output, so he's doing, like, like, content audits and helping think about, like, the inputs and prompts that generate good content.
So kind of, like, at our most basic level, what we're delivering.

I'm thinking about, like, the structure of the teams, how we think about scaling as, like, a startup rather than an agency, and how we manage kind of the client relationships and set expectations, contractual obligations, kind of closer to, like, revenue operations,
So it's a bit of a hybrid role in consideration of the fact that we're built on a product layer that could eventually become a SaaS service.
Right, okay.
So, can I ask what kind of things, like, you must, I mean, there must be a number of metrics that you're kind of being assessed on and that you're looking at to understand.
Yeah, so retention is, like, the main one.
So I am responsible for retention and renewal.
So customer health is kind of the metric that I own.
Yeah, satisfaction surveys or other kinds of customers.
We are, I'm writing the first satisfaction survey that will go out this week.
Up until this point, we've done, like, listening tours where myself and Marcel, the CEO, will.
Spend time with the kind of stakeholders on like a biannual basis.
Okay, okay.
Okay.
But from what you're creating now, you'll present the client for something and so they can just give you some feedback.
Is that right?
Yeah, yeah.
But I think like we still have a small enough book of business.
think we have like 40-ish clients that it is not crazy to say like if you want to schedule time with me and talk about like your deliverables or how we change work streams, things like that.
Like we can talk about that.
Yeah.
Okay.
Another question.
And this is, I'll just ask this because it's something that's in my head.
It's that on your homepage, one of the things that you promised to do is something like the language is something like tailor workflows or tailored.
I know what it says.
I've got it here.
I think.
Which, I think, feels like it speaks very much to what this role would be, custom built workflows.
And so, the reason why that stood out to me was because, first of all, like, oh, that sounds very much like what I'll be really deeply involved in.
Also, it made me think, I'd be really surprised if one of your clients saw that, and it kind of resonated with them.
But does it?
Is that something like that?
Yeah.
Let me show you what one of our workflows looks like.
I'll just pull one out.
Okay.
Sorry.
Well, this is just a
piece of one, but, so we have like a workflow library, that's a bunch of templates of AI, like, it's got steps that push towards AI prompts for different, like, steps of how to generate content.

So, this is like a very custom one, but we want to integrate expert quotes into an article, and rather than somebody searching for the quote and finding the best place to put it, you know, analyzing the scheme, theme scoring relevant, and inserting contextually appropriate quotes, and the AI will do that, but the workflow is, you know, they've given us a library of quotes that we can pull from.
The AI can analyze article themes, you talked about doing that for your brief, filter the quotes, score the relevance, select the best quotes, integrate them, and
And return the integrated article, so we have a different workflow that drafts the article, and then we have one that integrates the quotes into the workflow.
And that's like an example of a custom workflow that we'll build.
And that's another way that we think about building leverage.
So, 25 articles a week becomes a little bit less daunting when you're not having to manually pull the quote, make sure it's the right quote, da-da-da-da-da.
Cool.
So, oh, I had a question about that.
So, you were provided by the client with a library of quotes in this case.
Would there be a situation where you would invest in some research at, say, the start of a client relationship in order to gather that SME, or?
So, do client deep dives at the beginning of a relationship where we spend time with their product team or their product marketing team.
Sometimes we're spending time with, like, the CEO to talk about, like, the vision.
And then they're giving us, like, you know, sometimes they have, like...
Sort of competitor book, right?
So of like feature comparisons.
And so we can write like versus articles for like how our client compares to all of its competitors.
And we can write 10 articles about that if they give us that.
We're not doing that research on our own.
Like our work is all about leveraging like what the client has and kind of maximizing the output at scale.

So we're usually not like working with our ICP is not like a first time startup that doesn't know who their persona is and what their jobs to be done are and like doesn't have those case studies because it's really hard to build leverage from zero.
I understand.
Yeah.
Yeah.
for example, Flying Cat, we'd kind of work with a larger spectrum of client types.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And B, the case that we would perform that really deep research and internal and external SMEs.
Yeah.
But with GrowthX, you're saying in order to process in the kind of way that we want to, we need to be at a certain level already.
Yeah, like our, really where we excel is volume-based results, right?
So, like, also every article doesn't need to be perfect if you're publishing 50 looking for signals and then can prune or edit as you're seeing fit.

Have you, okay, just one final, and maybe you can't really answer this, but I'm just wondering, have you experienced any issues with, like, content bloat, where there's just, like, too much content and you need to, therefore, like, strip away a lot of the, a lot of that work in order to optimize overall performance?
Like, has that been...
Yeah, we have a...
I had that had like 6,000 pages.
And the first tranche of work that we did for them was pruning and refreshes.
It was no new work.
But we could, because we built them a custom workflow, we were publishing 50, we were refreshing 50 articles a week.
So we figured out what to prune.
And then we were refreshing the performant articles.
And so I think we ended up refreshing like 500 articles before we did any new work for them.
Yeah, okay.
Good.
right.
Andi.
Yeah.
It's been a great meeting.
really, really enjoyed it.
You too.
Thanks for your time, Sam.
Have a good one.
Okay.
Take care.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=386.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves managing 3-5 clients, delivering 5 articles/week per client using AI-powered content creation](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=405.0)
  - [Key skills: project management, organization, editorial taste, and adaptability in a startup environment](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
  - [GrowthX focuses on high-volume, AI-assisted content production for established clients with existing brand/product knowledge](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=2127.0)
  - [Retention and customer health are primary metrics for success](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1882.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview and Expectations

  - [Managing editor oversees 3-5 clients, aiming for 5 clients as company grows](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=405.0)
  - [Responsible for 15-25 pieces of content per week](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=942.0)
  - [Works with content managers and account strategists/directors](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=405.0)
  - [Focuses on meeting contractual obligations, strategy setting, and optimization](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=405.0)
  - [Key skills: project management, organization, and editorial judgment](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
  - [Role may evolve as the company grows, given its startup nature](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)

### Candidate Background and Experience

  - [Sam presents himself as a content editor who bridges gap between writers and clients](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=595.0)
  - [Experience in developing strategies, SERP analysis, and competitor analysis](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=662.0)
  - [Previous roles involved content management and project management](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=727.0)
  - [Familiar with tools like ClickUp, Productive, Google Sheets, and Slack for project management](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=873.0)

### Content Management and Workflow

  - [GrowthX uses a relentless delivery cadence of 5 articles per week per client](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=942.0)
  - [Sam suggests prioritizing bottlenecks (e.g., outline approvals) and batching work for client review](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=960.0)
  - [Emphasis on streamlining processes and understanding why edits might take longer than expected](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1137.0)
  - [Need to adjust workflows if content quality isn't meeting expectations efficiently](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1137.0)

### AI and Automation Experience

  - [Sam uses AI features in various tools, including Slight for SOP management](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1435.0)
  - [Primary use of ChatGPT for content creation, outline structuring, and analysis](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1435.0)
  - [Created custom GPTs for complex brief generation](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1552.0)
  - [Used AI in the application exercise to analyze options and interpret brand guidelines](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1584.0)

### GrowthX Operations and Metrics

  - [Andi's role as Head of Customer Operations focuses on team structure, scaling, and client relationship management](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1768.0)
  - [Main metric is retention and renewal, with customer health as a key indicator](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1882.0)
  - [Implementing first customer satisfaction survey, previously relied on listening tours with stakeholders](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1882.0)
  - [Custom workflows are a key differentiator, using AI to streamline content creation processes](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1962.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Sam to prepare a presentation for the next stage, detailing AI usage in the application exercise](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=1718.0)
  - [Andi to review Sam's qualifications against role requirements and team fit](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=2318.0)
  - [Potential follow-up on any additional questions or clarifications needed from either party](https://fathom.video/share/wXpF-Wub3Ld--v_LpKkcBmLzzsqHWLu9?tab=summary&timestamp=2318.0)
